Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This is a VMC wind and terrain day — a strong Pacific system is driving moisture into the Pacific Northwest by Monday, but the core of it stays well northwest of your route. Primary Risk: a developing low pressure center tracking into the northern Rockies/Great Plains by Mon 00Z introduces uncertainty about how much moisture and associated cloud cover reaches the Cascades and northern California en route, particularly the terrain crossing between Bend and the Bay Area. Based on current chart guidance, this looks like a MARGINAL GO — conditions are flyable but the forecast maturity is limited at 72+ hours and a Pacific trough is actively evolving. The single most important variable: watch whether the developing low tracks far enough south to push QPF and associated cloud decks into central Oregon and northern California on Monday.

Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

Sunday evening (Sun 12Z–00Z), the synoptic picture over the Pacific Northwest shows a high holding over the central Rockies and Great Plains with a clean, dry continental airmass over Oregon and northern California. That's the favorable picture. The concern is what's happening offshore — a Pacific trough is consolidating and pushing onshore into the Pacific Northwest. By Sun 12Z the 48-hr prog shows that system still well north of your route, but it's moving.

Sunday night check list:

  • Pull the updated 24-hr and 12-hr surface progs Sunday evening — look at how far south the low and its associated frontal band has tracked. If the trough axis is pushing into central Oregon by Monday morning, expect deteriorating ceilings along the Cascades.
  • Check QPF for central Oregon and northern California — specifically whether the green (0.10"+) QPF envelope has crept south of roughly 44°N. If it has, expect IMC along the Cascade range.
  • Check updated TAFs for KBDN and KSQL when they become available (12–18 hours prior to your departure). That's the definitive answer on what the departure and arrival airports are doing.
  • Check the Day-3 winds aloft (Mon 18Z) for the route — specifically 9,000 and 12,000 ft over central Oregon and northern California. If winds are running 40+ kt from the southwest, expect moderate turbulence over terrain.

Leaving KBDN — Departure at 2026-03-30 21:30Z

TAFs are not yet available for KBDN at this range — this is a 72-hour forecast and TAF coverage doesn't extend there. So we're working off the synoptic picture only.

Here's what the charts say about Bend at Monday 21:30Z (1:30 PM local Pacific Daylight Time):

  • The 60-hr prog (Mon 00Z) shows a developing low well to the northwest, with Bend still sitting in a relatively benign pressure gradient — isobars are not packed tightly over central Oregon at that time. The associated QPF from the Day 3 products shows minimal precipitation over central Oregon through Mon 06Z, with the bulk of moisture staying in the Pacific Northwest (Washington, northern Idaho).
  • Bend (elevation ~3,460 ft MSL) sits east of the Cascades in the high desert. Under a southwest flow ahead of a Pacific trough, expect possible mid and high cloud build from the west through the afternoon, but widespread low-level IMC at departure is not supported by current chart data. CONDITIONAL low ceilings are possible if the trough tracks farther south than currently forecast — that's your watch item.
  • Surface winds at KBDN: With the pressure gradient tightening on the backside of the inland high and ahead of the incoming trough, southwest winds 10–20 kt are a reasonable estimate. Runway 16/34 at KBDN — expect a quartering crosswind. Nothing alarming at this point, but verify when TAFs are available.
  • Climbout: With mostly dry air east of the Cascades, the climb through 12,000 ft should be VMC. CONDITIONAL mid-level cloud layers possible if moisture advects ahead of the trough — this is a watch item, not an expectation.

Pilot mental model for departure: Most likely a clean VFR departure out of Bend with increasing high clouds. If the trough tracks south overnight and there's QPF touching central Oregon on Monday morning charts, revisit this call before you go.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Icing

No icing charts (FIP/CIP) have been provided at this forecast range — they don't extend to 72 hours. So icing analysis is based on moisture gating from QPF and synoptic pattern only.

Moisture gating assessment: QPF Day 3 products show no meaningful precipitation over central Oregon or northern California during the Monday afternoon window. The moisture is concentrated in western Washington, the BC coast, and the northern Rockies. Central Oregon and the Sierra/Cascade passes are essentially dry in the Day 3 QPF envelope for the Monday flight window.

Icing classification: LOW CONFIDENCE — likely limited

  • No widespread moisture signal over the route. Structural icing at 12,000 ft is not supported by current QPF or synoptic data for this specific time window.
  • CONDITIONAL: If the trough tracks farther south and QPF pushes into central Oregon, you could encounter freezing levels and cloud in the 8,000–12,000 ft band over the Cascades. Freezing level in a March Pacific flow at this latitude is typically 7,000–9,000 ft in moist air, so 12,000 ft could put you in the ice in a saturated environment.
  • No SLD signal is present in available data. SLD not a current concern.
  • Dry air escape: descend below 8,000 ft east of the Cascades or continue west of the mountains into warmer maritime air.
AltitudeIcing Probability (current guidance)Notes
Below 8,000 ft MSLMinimalBelow likely freezing level in dry air
8,000–12,000 ft MSLLow (CONDITIONAL)Risk increases if trough pushes moisture south
12,000 ft MSL (planned)Low to Moderate (CONDITIONAL)Only if cloud/moisture present — not currently forecast
Above 12,000 ftDecreasingCould be above cloud tops in drier synoptic flow

Turbulence and Ride Quality

No GTG or G-AIRMET data has been provided at this range. Assessment is based on synoptic wind patterns and terrain.

Winds aloft: TAFs and winds aloft forecasts are not yet available for Monday. Based on synoptic pattern — a developing Pacific trough approaching from the northwest with the inland high eroding — expect southwest flow strengthening through Monday. A reasonable estimate for 12,000 ft over central Oregon and northern California is 25–40 kt from the southwest. The exact value matters a lot here and needs verification when Monday winds aloft data becomes available.

Wind → turbulence mapping for this route:

  • 25–30 kt at 12,000 ft: Light to moderate terrain-driven turbulence POSSIBLE over the Cascades and Siskiyous. Ride quality: light chop to occasional light turbulence. Manageable.
  • 35–40 kt at 12,000 ft: Mountain wave development LIKELY over the Cascades. Moderate turbulence POSSIBLE, particularly in rotor zones on the lee side of ridges. This is the range where you want to think about altitude.
  • >40 kt at 12,000 ft: Moderate turbulence likely over terrain. Consider staying below 10,000 ft on the east side or climbing above 14,000 ft to get above the worst rotor zone — neither is great for the whole route.

Why this happens: Southwest flow over the Cascades (oriented roughly NW-SE) hits the ridgeline at an angle, generating lee-side waves and rotor zones on the eastern slopes. At 12,000 ft you're right in the primary turbulence band when winds are strong.

Altitude strategy:

  • Best altitude band: 10,000–12,000 ft in moderate wind scenario (25–35 kt). Gets you above the worst mechanical turbulence near terrain while keeping you below the most intense wave activity aloft.
  • Worst altitude band: 7,000–9,000 ft in strong wind — this is the rotor zone on the Cascade lee side.
  • Escape option: If you pick up unexpected moderate turbulence over the Cascades, the best move is to climb to 14,000+ ft to get above the rotor zone. If icing is present at altitude, descend and divert west — don't try to thread the needle.

Getting Into KSQL

TAFs for KSQL are not yet available at this range. Estimated arrival is approximately Monday 23:50Z (3:50 PM Pacific Daylight Time).

San Carlos arrival picture based on synoptic data:

  • The Day 3 QPF shows essentially zero QPF over the Bay Area for the Monday afternoon window. The frontal system and associated moisture stays well to the north. This is a dry airmass arrival.
  • The primary arrival concern at KSQL in a southwest synoptic flow is the Bay Area marine layer. In late March with afternoon timing (~4 PM local), marine stratus is a classic threat. However, with a Pacific trough approaching and a post-frontal or pre-frontal onshore flow, low ceilings are CONDITIONAL — afternoon heating typically burns the marine layer back, but if the onshore flow is strong enough, fog and stratus can persist or redevelop in the afternoon.
  • KSQL sits on the Peninsula between San Francisco Bay and the hills. Approach from the southeast preferred in strong southwest flow — terrain on the west side can distort winds on final. Expect a variable crosswind from the northwest to southwest at the surface, estimated 10–20 kt. Do not assume winds aloft translate directly to the runway — Bay Area terrain channels surface winds significantly.
  • Runway wind estimate: UNCERTAIN — terrain-modified. Likely northwest to west at 10–18 kt surface. Treat as variable until TAFs are available.

Good alternates: KSJC (San Jose) — ILS approaches, good alternates in coastal IMC. KOAK (Oakland) — more sheltered from coastal marine layer. File IFR with these as your alternates.

Block time estimate: KBDN to KSQL is roughly 320 nm. At 12,000 ft with 25–35 kt headwind component from the southwest, expect 2 hrs 20 min to 2 hrs 40 min. Fuel planning: account for the headwind range.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — based on current chart guidance, the route is flyable Monday afternoon. The Pacific trough is the wildcard, and at 72+ hours it's not pinned down enough to give a confident GO. Here's the bottom line:

  • Current forecast keeps the moisture and frontal weather well north of your route through Monday afternoon.
  • The ride at 12,000 ft will have some terrain-driven turbulence on the Cascade crossing — expect light to moderate chop, not a smooth ride.
  • No widespread IMC or icing is currently forecast for your route and time window.
  • The margin of safety is reasonable but not generous. File IFR — not because IMC is expected, but because the terrain routing and evolving synoptic pattern warrant the safety margin.

What changes this call

  • QPF pushes south of 44°N over central Oregon on Sunday night's updated charts — that means moisture reaching your route; re-evaluate departure and expect possible icing and IMC on the Cascade crossing.
  • Winds aloft at 12,000 ft exceed 40 kt from the southwest over central Oregon — upgrade turbulence risk to moderate; consider altitude change or delay.
  • KSQL TAF shows BKN010 or lower on Monday afternoon — marine layer is winning; divert planning to KSJC becomes primary, not backup.
  • Surface winds at KBDN exceed 20G30 kt crosswind on departure TAF — verify aircraft crosswind limits before committing.

If You Go — Do This

  • Sunday evening (around 03:00–06:00Z Monday): Pull updated surface progs, QPF Day 2/3, and the latest winds aloft forecast for Mon 18Z. This is your last real chance to see the updated trough track before your go/no-go decision crystallizes.
  • Monday morning (4–6 hours before departure): Get the TAFs for KBDN and KSQL as soon as they cover your flight window. Check METAR at KBDN for actual conditions. Pull PIREP data for any reports over the Cascades or northern California mountains.
  • Altitude: Plan 12,000 ft. If updated winds aloft show 40+ kt, consider requesting 10,000 ft on the east side of the Cascades and climb to 12,000 once you're in the Sacramento Valley. If turbulence is worse than expected over the Cascades, climb to 14,000+ to escape the rotor zone (confirm oxygen requirement for your aircraft/passengers).
  • Fuel: Plan for the headwind scenario — budget for 35 kt headwind component and add reserves. Bay Area diversions burn extra fuel.
  • Route: KBDN → J1/V105 corridor southwest through northern California → Bay Area. Stay east of the Cascade crest as long as practical to minimize terrain exposure on climbout. Cross the mountains when you have altitude and margin.
  • Alternates: File KSJC as alternate. Know the KOAK approach as backup. Both have ILS approaches and better IMC margins than KSQL.
  • File IFR. This route crosses terrain and the synoptic pattern is evolving. IFR gives you ATC eyes, separation, and options. There's no good reason not to.

What Forecasters Are Saying

Note: No AFD was directly provided in this brief. The following reflects what the chart package implies forecasters would be highlighting at this range:

  • Pacific trough timing uncertainty: At 72+ hours, the track and speed of the offshore system carries meaningful uncertainty — the trough could arrive 6–12 hours earlier or later than currently shown, which changes the Monday afternoon picture significantly.
  • Low confidence on moisture extent: Whether the associated cloud/QPF envelope reaches central Oregon by Monday afternoon is the key forecast question. Forecasters would likely flag this as the main uncertainty.
  • Northern California and Bay Area dry for now: Current guidance confidently keeps the frontal precip north of roughly 42°N through Monday evening. Southern Oregon and Bay Area are not in the QPF envelope.
  • Trigger condition: If the trough accelerates or the low tracks 100–150 nm farther south than currently forecast, the entire picture shifts — more cloud, possible icing, and deteriorating Bay Area conditions.

Confidence

MEDIUM — limited forecast maturity at 72+ hours.

Here's the honest assessment: the synoptic charts are consistent in keeping the bad weather north of your route through Monday afternoon, and the QPF supports that story. That's a reasonable foundation. But at 72 hours, trough track forecasts carry meaningful error margins — particularly in the Pacific Northwest where model performance on offshore system tracks varies. The forecast maturity is limited at this range.

What's solid: the Bay Area arrival looks clean, no widespread icing signal, no convective threat along the route. What's uncertain: exactly how far south the trough pushes by Monday afternoon and whether it brings meaningful cloud and moisture to central Oregon and the northern California mountains. That uncertainty alone keeps this at MEDIUM confidence.

Re-evaluate Sunday night with fresh charts. By Monday morning with TAFs in hand, you should be able to make a confident final call.
